seo.title: JavaScript Arrays - map, filter, reduce, sort, spread, destructuring | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master JavaScript arrays: push, pop, map, filter, reduce, sort, find, includes, spread operator, destructuring, and method chaining. Includes 58 practice questions with real data processing challenges.
seo.keywords: javascript arrays, javascript map filter reduce, array methods javascript, javascript array tutorial, javascript spread operator, array destructuring javascript, javascript sort array, javascript forEach, javascript find findIndex, javascript array chaining

content.what:
What Are Arrays in JavaScript?

An array is an ordered collection of values stored in a single variable. Instead of creating separate variables for 100 student names, you store them all in one array and access any of them by position (index).

Arrays in JavaScript are flexible. They can hold any type of data: numbers, strings, booleans, objects, and even other arrays. They can also grow and shrink dynamically - you do not need to specify a size when creating one.

const fruits = ["apple", "mango", "banana"];
const scores = [85, 92, 78, 95, 88];
const mixed = ["Aarav", 16, true, null];

Each element has an index, starting at 0. The first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on. You access elements using square brackets: fruits[0] gives "apple".
content.why:
Why Are Arrays Important?

Arrays are one of the most used data structures in all of programming. Here is why you need to master them:

1. Real Data Comes in Lists

Student names, product prices, quiz scores, blog posts, search results - almost all real data is a collection of items. Arrays are how JavaScript stores and works with these collections.

2. Powerful Built-in Methods

JavaScript arrays come with dozens of built-in methods like map(), filter(), reduce(), sort(), and find(). These methods let you transform, filter, search, and process data in just one or two lines of code.

3. Building Dynamic UIs

Every time you see a list of products on Amazon, search results on Google, or posts on Instagram, an array of data was processed and turned into HTML elements. Arrays are the backbone of dynamic web pages.

4. API Data Processing

When you fetch data from an API (like weather data or movie listings), it almost always comes as an array of objects. You need array methods to filter, sort, and display this data.

5. Foundation for Advanced Patterns

Method chaining, functional programming, state management in React, and data pipelines all rely heavily on array methods. If you master arrays, everything else becomes easier.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. Creating and Accessing Arrays

You create arrays using square brackets. Access elements by their index (starting at 0). Modify elements by assigning to an index.

const colors = ["red", "green", "blue"];
console.log(colors[0]);    // "red"
console.log(colors[2]);    // "blue"
console.log(colors.length); // 3

colors[1] = "yellow"; // Modify an element
console.log(colors);  // ["red", "yellow", "blue"]

Note: even though the array is declared with const, you can still modify its contents. The const only prevents reassigning the variable, not changing the array itself.

2. Adding and Removing Elements

JavaScript provides methods to add and remove elements from both ends of an array:

const arr = [1, 2, 3];

arr.push(4);     // Add to end: [1, 2, 3, 4]
arr.pop();       // Remove from end: [1, 2, 3]
arr.unshift(0);  // Add to start: [0, 1, 2, 3]
arr.shift();     // Remove from start: [1, 2, 3]

push and pop work on the end. unshift and shift work on the start. push and unshift return the new length. pop and shift return the removed element.

3. splice and slice

splice changes the array in place - it can remove, replace, or insert elements at any position:

const arr = ["a", "b", "c", "d", "e"];

arr.splice(2, 1);          // Remove 1 element at index 2: ["a", "b", "d", "e"]
arr.splice(1, 0, "x");     // Insert "x" at index 1: ["a", "x", "b", "d", "e"]
arr.splice(0, 2, "y", "z"); // Replace 2 elements at start: ["y", "z", "b", "d", "e"]

slice creates a new array from a portion of the original, without modifying it:

const arr = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
console.log(arr.slice(1, 4)); // [2, 3, 4] (index 1 to 3)
console.log(arr.slice(2));    // [3, 4, 5] (index 2 to end)
console.log(arr);             // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] (unchanged)

4. Searching: indexOf, includes, find, findIndex

const nums = [10, 20, 30, 40, 50];

nums.indexOf(30);     // 2 (index of first match)
nums.includes(20);    // true (does it exist?)
nums.find(n => n > 25);      // 30 (first element passing the test)
nums.findIndex(n => n > 25); // 2 (index of first element passing the test)

indexOf and includes work with simple values. find and findIndex take a callback function, making them powerful for complex conditions.

5. map - Transform Every Element

map() creates a new array by calling a function on every element of the original:

const prices = [100, 200, 300];
const withTax = prices.map(price => price * 1.18);
console.log(withTax); // [118, 236, 354]

The original array is unchanged. map always returns a new array of the same length. It is the most common array method in web development.

6. filter - Select Elements

filter() creates a new array with only the elements that pass a test:

const scores = [85, 42, 67, 91, 38, 73];
const passing = scores.filter(s => s >= 50);
console.log(passing); // [85, 67, 91, 73]

The callback must return true or false. Elements where the callback returns true are included; others are excluded.

7. reduce - Accumulate to a Single Value

reduce() processes all elements and reduces them to a single value (a number, string, object, etc.):

const nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5];
const sum = nums.reduce((acc, curr) => acc + curr, 0);
console.log(sum); // 15

The callback takes an accumulator (running result) and the current element. The second argument to reduce (0) is the initial value of the accumulator. Step by step: 0+1=1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10, 10+5=15.

8. sort - Ordering Elements

sort() sorts the array in place (modifies the original). By default, it sorts elements as strings, which causes problems with numbers:

const nums = [40, 100, 1, 5, 25];
nums.sort(); // [1, 100, 25, 40, 5] - WRONG! (string comparison)

// Correct: use a compare function
nums.sort((a, b) => a - b); // [1, 5, 25, 40, 100] - ascending
nums.sort((a, b) => b - a); // [100, 40, 25, 5, 1] - descending

The compare function returns a negative number (a comes first), zero (equal), or positive number (b comes first).

9. forEach - Do Something for Each Element

forEach() calls a function for each element but does NOT return a new array. Use it for side effects like logging or DOM manipulation:

const names = ["Aarav", "Priya", "Rohan"];
names.forEach((name, index) => {
  console.log(index + 1 + ". " + name);
});

10. every and some

every() checks if ALL elements pass a test. some() checks if AT LEAST ONE element passes:

const ages = [15, 17, 14, 16, 18];
ages.every(a => a >= 18); // false (not all are 18+)
ages.some(a => a >= 18);  // true (at least one is 18+)

11. Spread Operator [...] and Array.from()

The spread operator (...) expands an array into individual elements. It is great for copying and merging arrays:

const a = [1, 2, 3];
const b = [4, 5, 6];
const combined = [...a, ...b]; // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
const copy = [...a];           // [1, 2, 3] (shallow copy)

Array.from() creates an array from array-like objects:

Array.from("hello");     // ["h", "e", "l", "l", "o"]
Array.from({length: 5}, (_, i) => i * 2); // [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

12. flat and flatMap

flat() flattens nested arrays. flatMap() maps and flattens in one step:

const nested = [[1, 2], [3, 4], [5, 6]];
nested.flat(); // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]

const sentences = ["hello world", "goodbye moon"];
sentences.flatMap(s => s.split(" ")); // ["hello", "world", "goodbye", "moon"]

13. Method Chaining

Since methods like map, filter, and sort return arrays, you can chain them together:

const students = [
  { name: "Aarav", score: 85 },
  { name: "Priya", score: 42 },
  { name: "Rohan", score: 91 },
  { name: "Diya", score: 67 }
];

const topScorers = students
  .filter(s => s.score >= 60)
  .sort((a, b) => b.score - a.score)
  .map(s => s.name);

console.log(topScorers); // ["Rohan", "Aarav", "Diya"]

This reads like a pipeline: filter out low scores, sort by score descending, then extract just the names. Chaining is extremely common in real JavaScript.

14. Destructuring Arrays

Destructuring lets you unpack array values into individual variables:

const [first, second, third] = ["apple", "mango", "banana"];
console.log(first);  // "apple"
console.log(second); // "mango"

// Skip elements
const [a, , c] = [1, 2, 3];
console.log(a, c); // 1 3

// Rest pattern
const [head, ...tail] = [10, 20, 30, 40];
console.log(head); // 10
console.log(tail); // [20, 30, 40]

codeExamples.title: Creating, Accessing, and Modifying Arrays
codeExamples.explanation: Arrays are zero-indexed: the first element is at index 0. Access the last element using arr[arr.length - 1]. You can modify elements by assigning to an index, even with const (const prevents reassignment of the variable, not mutation of the array).

codeExamples.title: push, pop, shift, unshift, splice
codeExamples.explanation: push adds to the end, shift removes from the start (like a real queue). splice(index, deleteCount, ...items) is the Swiss army knife: it can insert, remove, or replace elements at any position.

codeExamples.title: map, filter, and reduce
codeExamples.explanation: map transforms every element (adds 5 to each mark). filter keeps only elements passing a condition (marks >= 50). reduce compresses the array into a single value (sum of all marks). None of these modify the original array.

codeExamples.title: Method Chaining with Real Data
codeExamples.explanation: This chain first filters products that are in stock, then filters by price, sorts by price ascending, and finally maps to display strings. Each method returns a new array, so you can keep chaining. This is how real e-commerce sites process product data.
codeExamples.output:
Affordable in-stock items:
- Headphones (Rs 3000)
- Watch (Rs 8000)
- Tablet (Rs 25000)

codeExamples.title: sort with Compare Function
codeExamples.explanation: Default sort() converts everything to strings, which gives wrong results for numbers (100 comes before 25 alphabetically). Always use a compare function for numbers: (a, b) => a - b for ascending, (a, b) => b - a for descending. We use [...nums] to copy the array before sorting since sort modifies in place.

codeExamples.title: Spread Operator, Array.from, and Destructuring
codeExamples.explanation: The spread operator ... unpacks arrays. Use it to merge arrays or create copies. Array.from creates arrays from array-like objects or with a mapping function. Destructuring unpacks array values into variables. The rest pattern ...rest collects remaining elements.
codeExamples.output:
Full stack: ["HTML", "CSS", "JavaScript", "Node", "Python", "Java"]
Evens: [2, 4, 6, 8, 10]
Gold: Aarav
Silver: Priya
Rest: ["Diya", "Vikram"]

codeExamples.title: find, findIndex, every, some
codeExamples.explanation: find returns the first element matching the condition. findIndex returns its index. every checks if ALL elements match (returns false if even one fails). some checks if ANY element matches (returns true if at least one passes).
codeExamples.output:
First topper: Aarav
First fail at index: 3
All passed: false
Any above 90: true

codeExamples.title: flat, flatMap, and Real Data Processing
codeExamples.explanation: flat() flattens one level of nesting by default. flatMap() is like calling map() then flat(1) in one step. The last example uses flatMap to get all subjects from all students, then a Set to remove duplicates, and spread to convert back to an array.

commonMistakes.title: Sorting Numbers Without a Compare Function
commonMistakes.error: Numbers are sorted as strings. 100 comes before 25 because "1" < "2" alphabetically.
commonMistakes.explanation: The default sort() converts elements to strings and sorts them alphabetically. For numbers, you MUST pass a compare function: (a, b) => a - b for ascending order. This is one of the most common JavaScript gotchas.

commonMistakes.title: Confusing map and forEach
commonMistakes.error: forEach returns undefined. It does not create a new array.
commonMistakes.explanation: forEach is for side effects (like logging or DOM updates). It always returns undefined. map is for transforming data - it returns a new array with the results. Use map when you need a new array, forEach when you just want to do something with each element.

commonMistakes.title: Forgetting That sort Mutates the Original Array
commonMistakes.error: sort modifies the original array. Both variables point to the same sorted array.
commonMistakes.explanation: sort() modifies the array in place. If you need to keep the original, spread it into a new array first: [...original].sort(). Methods like map, filter, and slice create new arrays, but sort, splice, push, and pop mutate the original.

commonMistakes.title: Using Wrong Initial Value in reduce
commonMistakes.error: Without an initial value, reduce fails on empty arrays.
commonMistakes.explanation: Always provide an initial value as the second argument to reduce. Without it, reduce uses the first element as the initial value, which fails on empty arrays. For sum, use 0. For product, use 1. For string concatenation, use "".

commonMistakes.title: Expecting indexOf to Work with Objects
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
const students = [{ name: "Aarav" }, { name: "Priya" }];
console.log(students.indexOf({ name: "Aarav" })); // -1 (not found!)
commonMistakes.error: indexOf compares by reference, not by value. Two different objects with the same content are not equal.
commonMistakes.explanation: In JavaScript, objects are compared by reference (memory address), not by their content. { name: "Aarav" } === { name: "Aarav" } is false because they are two different objects. Use findIndex with a callback to search by property values.

summary: Arrays are ordered collections of values, accessed by zero-based index. They can hold any data type and grow/shrink dynamically.
push/pop add/remove from the end. unshift/shift add/remove from the start. splice can insert, remove, or replace at any position.
slice creates a new array from a portion without modifying the original. splice modifies the original array in place.
map transforms every element and returns a new array. Use it when you need to convert data (like adding tax to prices or extracting names).
filter creates a new array with only elements that pass a test. Use it to select or exclude items based on a condition.
reduce processes all elements into a single value. Always provide an initial value as the second argument to avoid errors with empty arrays.
sort modifies the array in place. For numbers, always use a compare function: (a, b) => a - b for ascending. Spread into a copy to preserve the original.
find returns the first matching element. findIndex returns its index. every checks if ALL match. some checks if ANY match.
The spread operator [...arr] copies arrays, merges arrays, and converts iterables to arrays. Array.from creates arrays from array-like objects.
Method chaining (filter then sort then map) is a powerful pattern for data processing. Each method returns a new array, enabling the chain.
Destructuring unpacks array values into variables. The rest pattern (...rest) collects remaining elements into a new array.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the output of the following code?
const arr = [10, 20, 30];
console.log(arr[1]);
topicSpecific.hint: Arrays are zero-indexed. Index 1 is the second element.
topicSpecific.explanation: Index 0 is 10, index 1 is 20, index 2 is 30. So arr[1] returns 20.

topicSpecific.hint: push adds an element to the end.
topicSpecific.explanation: push(4) adds 4 to the end of the array. The array becomes [1, 2, 3, 4].

topicSpecific.hint: length is 3. The last index is length - 1.
topicSpecific.explanation: The array has 3 elements. arr[arr.length - 1] is arr[2] which is "c". This is the standard way to access the last element.

topicSpecific.hint: map transforms each element and returns a new array.
topicSpecific.explanation: map calls the callback on each element: 5*2=10, 10*2=20, 15*2=30. Returns a new array [10, 20, 30]. The original array is unchanged.

topicSpecific.hint: filter keeps elements where the callback returns true.
topicSpecific.explanation: filter tests each element. 1%2=1 (false), 2%2=0 (true), 3%2=1 (false), 4%2=0 (true), 5%2=1 (false). Only 2 and 4 pass the test.

topicSpecific.hint: includes returns true if the value is in the array.
topicSpecific.explanation: 20 is in the array, so includes(20) returns true. 25 is not in the array, so includes(25) returns false.
reduce starts with acc=0. Step by step: 0+1=1, 1+2=3, 3+3=6, 6+4=10, 10+5=15. The final accumulated value is 15.

topicSpecific.hint: Spreading creates a copy. Sort modifies the copy, not the original.
topicSpecific.explanation: [...arr] creates a copy. sort modifies that copy, not the original. So sorted is [1,1,3,4,5] but arr remains [3,1,4,1,5].

topicSpecific.hint: slice(start, end) extracts from start up to (not including) end.
topicSpecific.explanation: slice(1, 4) extracts elements at indices 1, 2, 3 (not 4): [2, 3, 4]. slice(3) extracts from index 3 to the end: [4, 5]. The original array is unchanged.

topicSpecific.hint: find returns the FIRST element that matches.
topicSpecific.explanation: find returns the first element where the callback returns true. It checks Aarav (72 > 80? No), then Priya (91 > 80? Yes). It returns Priya's object immediately without checking Rohan.

topicSpecific.hint: Destructuring with rest pattern collects remaining elements.
topicSpecific.explanation: a gets the first element (10), b gets the second (20), and ...rest collects everything else into an array [30, 40, 50].

topicSpecific.hint: Filter even numbers first, then square each one.
topicSpecific.explanation: First, filter keeps even numbers: [2, 4, 6]. Then map squares each: [4, 16, 36]. This is method chaining - the output of filter feeds into map.

topicSpecific.hint: reduce finds the longest word by comparing lengths.
topicSpecific.explanation: Starting with "" (length 0): "hello" (5 > 0, keep "hello"), "world" (5 > 5? No, keep "hello"), "javascript" (10 > 5, keep "javascript"). The longest word is "javascript".

topicSpecific.hint: reduce builds an object that groups numbers into even and odd arrays.
topicSpecific.explanation: The accumulator starts as { even: [], odd: [] }. Each number is pushed into the appropriate array based on whether it is even or odd. This is a grouping pattern using reduce.

topicSpecific.hint: Spread both arrays, use a Set to remove duplicates, spread back to array.
topicSpecific.explanation: [...a, ...b] merges into [1,2,3,3,4,5]. new Set() removes duplicates: {1,2,3,4,5}. Spreading the Set back gives [1,2,3,4,5].

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between map and forEach? When would you use each one?
topicSpecific.hint: One returns a new array, the other returns undefined.
topicSpecific.answer: map transforms each element and returns a new array with the results. forEach runs a function for each element but returns undefined. Use map when you need to transform data into a new array. Use forEach for side effects like logging, updating DOM elements, or other operations where you do not need a return value.
topicSpecific.explanation: Example: [1,2,3].map(n => n*2) returns [2,4,6]. [1,2,3].forEach(n => console.log(n)) logs each number but returns undefined. A common mistake is trying to use forEach to create a new array.

topicSpecific.hint: every checks ALL elements. some checks if ANY element passes.
topicSpecific.explanation: every returns false because not all ages are >= 18 (12, 15, 16 fail). some returns true because at least one age is >= 18 (18 and 21 pass).

topicSpecific.hint: pop removes and returns the last element.
topicSpecific.explanation: pop() removes the last element ("c") and returns it. The array is now ["a", "b"].

topicSpecific.hint: flat flattens one level of nesting. Then reduce sums them all.
topicSpecific.explanation: flat() turns [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]] into [1,2,3,4,5,6]. Then reduce sums all values: 1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21.

topicSpecific.hint: findIndex returns the INDEX of the first matching element.
topicSpecific.explanation: findIndex tests each element: 1>3? No. 2>3? No. 3>3? No. 4>3? Yes, at index 3. Returns 3 (the index, not the value).

mixed.hint: unshift adds to the start of the array.
mixed.explanation: unshift("w") adds "w" to the beginning. The array becomes ["w", "x", "y", "z"].

mixed.hint: indexOf returns the index or -1 if not found.
mixed.explanation: "Priya" is at index 1. "Diya" is not in the array, so indexOf returns -1.

mixed.hint: Filter first (>= 20), then divide each by 10.
mixed.explanation: Filter keeps [20, 30, 40, 50] (all >= 20). Map divides each by 10: [2, 3, 4, 5].

mixed.hint: Spread unpacks arrays. You can add extra elements too.
mixed.explanation: ...a gives 1, 2. ...b gives 3, 4. Then 5 is added. The result is [1, 2, 3, 4, 5].

mixed.hint: map transforms each string to uppercase.
mixed.explanation: map calls toUpperCase() on each string and returns a new array with the uppercase versions.

mixed.hint: Filter, then check the length of the filtered array.
mixed.explanation: filter keeps scores >= 50: [85, 91, 67]. The length of that array is 3. Three students passed.

mixed.hint: Filter >= 85, sort descending by marks, extract names.
mixed.explanation: Filter keeps students with marks >= 85: Aarav(85), Priya(92), Diya(95). Sort descending: Diya(95), Priya(92), Aarav(85). Map extracts names: ["Diya", "Priya", "Aarav"].

mixed.hint: reduce compares each word's length to find the longest.
mixed.explanation: Starting with "cat": "elephant"(8) > "cat"(3) -> "elephant". "dog"(3) < "elephant"(8) -> "elephant". "hippopotamus"(12) > "elephant"(8) -> "hippopotamus". The longest word is "hippopotamus".

mixed.question:
What is the output?
const arr = Array.from({ length: 5 }, (_, i) => i * i);
console.log(arr);
mixed.hint: Array.from with a mapping function. The second parameter (i) is the index.
mixed.explanation: Array.from creates an array of length 5. The mapping function receives each index (0,1,2,3,4) and returns its square: 0, 1, 4, 9, 16.

mixed.hint: reduce groups items by category into an object.
mixed.explanation: This is a groupBy pattern. The accumulator starts as an empty object. For each item, it creates an array for the category (if it does not exist) and pushes the name into it. The result groups items by their category.

mixed.question: Explain the difference between slice and splice. Which one modifies the original array?
mixed.answer: slice(start, end) creates a new array from a portion of the original without modifying it. splice(start, deleteCount, ...items) modifies the original array by removing, replacing, or inserting elements at a specified position. splice modifies the original, slice does not.
mixed.explanation: Think of it this way: slice is like photocopying some pages from a book (original stays intact). Splice is like cutting pages out of the book and optionally inserting new ones (original is changed).

mixed.hint: shift removes and returns the first element.
mixed.explanation: shift() removes the first element (1) and returns it. The array becomes [2, 3, 4].

mixed.hint: flatMap maps then flattens one level.
mixed.explanation: map would give [["hello","world"],["foo","bar","baz"]]. flatMap flattens that one level to ["hello","world","foo","bar","baz"].

mcqs.question: What index does the first element of an array have?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Arrays in JavaScript are zero-indexed. The first element is always at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on.

mcqs.question: Which method adds an element to the END of an array?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. push() adds to the end. unshift() adds to the start. pop() removes from the end. shift() removes from the start.

mcqs.question: Which method returns a new array with transformed elements?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. map() calls a function on each element and returns a new array with the results. forEach returns undefined. filter returns selected elements. reduce returns a single value.

mcqs.question: What does [1, 2, 3].includes(2) return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. includes() returns a boolean: true if the value is found in the array, false if not. Since 2 is in the array, it returns true.

mcqs.question: What does arr.pop() return?

options.A: The new length of the array

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. pop() removes the last element from the array and returns that removed element. push() is the one that returns the new length.

mcqs.question: What is the default sorting behavior of arr.sort()?

options.C: Converts elements to strings and sorts alphabetically

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. By default, sort() converts elements to strings and sorts them by Unicode code points (alphabetically). This is why [40, 100, 1] sorts as [1, 100, 40] - because "100" comes before "40" as strings.

mcqs.question: What does filter() return if no elements match the condition?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. filter() always returns an array. If no elements pass the test, it returns an empty array []. It never returns null, undefined, or false.

mcqs.question: Which method modifies the original array?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. sort() modifies the original array in place. map(), filter(), and slice() all return new arrays without changing the original.

mcqs.question: What does the spread operator [...arr] do?

options.B: Creates a shallow copy of the array

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. [...arr] creates a shallow copy of the array. It does not reverse (option A), flatten deeply nested arrays (option C), or remove duplicates (option D - use a Set for that).

mcqs.question: What is the second argument to reduce()?

options.C: The initial value of the accumulator

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. reduce(callback, initialValue) takes a callback as the first argument and an optional initial value for the accumulator as the second. Always provide an initial value to avoid errors with empty arrays.

mcqs.question: What is the result of [1, 2, 3].map(n => n > 1)?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. map transforms each element using the callback. 1 > 1 is false, 2 > 1 is true, 3 > 1 is true. The result is [false, true, true]. If you wanted [2, 3], you would use filter instead.

mcqs.question: What does Array.from({length: 3}, (_, i) => i) return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Array.from creates an array of length 3 and calls the mapping function for each index. The function receives the element (undefined, which we ignore with _) and the index. It returns [0, 1, 2].

mcqs.question: What is the difference between find() and filter()?

options.A: find returns an array, filter returns a single element
options.B: find returns the first matching element, filter returns all matching elements as an array
options.D: find searches by index, filter searches by value

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. find returns the first element that matches (or undefined if none match). filter returns a new array with ALL elements that match (or an empty array if none match). find stops at the first match; filter checks every element.

mcqs.question: What does [1, [2, [3]]].flat() return?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. By default, flat() flattens one level of nesting. The outer array [1, [2, [3]]] becomes [1, 2, [3]]. The inner [3] is not flattened because it is at depth 2. Use flat(2) or flat(Infinity) to flatten deeper.

mcqs.question: What does arr.length return?

options.A: The last index of the array

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. length returns the total number of elements in the array. The last index is length - 1 (option A). JavaScript arrays do not have a fixed capacity (option D).

mcqs.question: How do you correctly sort numbers in ascending order?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. (a, b) => a - b sorts numbers ascending. If a < b, the result is negative (a comes first). If a > b, the result is positive (b comes first). Option B sorts descending.

codingChallenges.title: Find the Maximum Value
codingChallenges.description: Aarav has the array [23, 67, 12, 89, 45, 91, 34]. Write a program using reduce to find the maximum value without using Math.max.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use reduce. Do not use Math.max or sort.

codingChallenges.title: Remove Duplicates
codingChallenges.description: Priya has the array [1, 3, 5, 3, 7, 1, 9, 5]. Write a program to remove duplicates and return a sorted array.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use the Set object and spread operator.

codingChallenges.title: Student Grade Report
codingChallenges.description: Given an array of student objects [{name: "Aarav", marks: 85}, {name: "Priya", marks: 42}, {name: "Rohan", marks: 91}, {name: "Diya", marks: 67}, {name: "Vikram", marks: 38}], use method chaining to: (1) filter students who passed (marks >= 50), (2) sort by marks descending, (3) map to strings like "Rohan: 91".
codingChallenges.constraints: Use filter, sort, and map chained together.

codingChallenges.title: Group By Category
codingChallenges.description: Kavya has an array of products: [{name: "Apple", type: "fruit"}, {name: "Carrot", type: "vegetable"}, {name: "Mango", type: "fruit"}, {name: "Potato", type: "vegetable"}, {name: "Banana", type: "fruit"}]. Use reduce to group them by type.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use reduce with an object as the accumulator.

codingChallenges.title: Flatten and Sum Nested Arrays
codingChallenges.description: Arjun has the nested array [[10, 20], [30, 40], [50]]. Write a program that flattens it and calculates the sum using flat and reduce.

codingChallenges.title: Word Frequency Counter
codingChallenges.description: Meera has the string "the cat sat on the mat the cat". Write a program that splits the string into words and uses reduce to count the frequency of each word.

codingChallenges.title: Intersection of Two Arrays
codingChallenges.description: Rohan has two arrays: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] and [3, 4, 5, 6, 7]. Write a program to find the common elements (intersection) and the elements unique to each array (difference).
codingChallenges.sampleOutput:
Common: [3, 4, 5]
Only in A: [1, 2]
Only in B: [6, 7]

codingChallenges.title: Build Product Cards from Data
codingChallenges.description: Diya has an array of products: [{name: "Laptop", price: 45000}, {name: "Phone", price: 15000}, {name: "Watch", price: 8000}]. Use map to generate HTML card strings for each product using template literals, then join them together.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: (Three HTML card divs with product name and price)
codingChallenges.constraints: Use map and template literals. Generate valid HTML strings.

codingChallenges.title: Custom Array Method: chunk
codingChallenges.description: Write a function chunk(arr, size) that splits an array into groups of the given size. For example, chunk([1,2,3,4,5], 2) should return [[1,2], [3,4], [5]].
codingChallenges.constraints: Use a loop with slice. Return a new array of arrays.
